currency.name: Namibian Dollar
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 150 NAD (South African rand also accepted everywhere)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Namibia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, in the south-west. Namibia has a long Atlantic coastline and borders South Africa, Botswana, Zambia and Angola 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Namibia?
mapQuestions.answer: Windhoek, in the centre of the country. Around 350,000 people, mountains all around. One of the cleanest and quietest capitals in Africa.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Namibia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: English is the official language, but most Namibians grow up speaking Afrikaans, Oshiwambo, German or one of about 30 local languages. Some German is left over from colonial times.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Namib Desert famous for?
mapQuestions.answer: The world's OLDEST desert (55 to 80 million years old), with some of the tallest sand dunes on Earth (over 300 m), and dramatic orange dunes that meet the cold Atlantic Ocean.

mapQuestions.question: How empty is Namibia?
mapQuestions.answer: Namibia has only about 3 people per square kilometre, one of the lowest population densities of any country on Earth. The UK has about 280 per square km.

foods.name: Game meat (kudu, gemsbok, springbok)
foods.description: Namibia's farms and game reserves serve antelope steaks at almost every restaurant. Lean, slightly sweet, deeply flavourful.

foods.name: Pap
foods.description: Stiff white maize porridge eaten with stew. The southern African staple, same as ugali / sadza / nsima further north.

foods.name: Braai (BBQ)
foods.description: The Namibian and South African weekend ritual: lamb chops, boerewors sausage and chicken cooked over wood embers, eaten outdoors with friends.

foods.name: Walvis Bay oysters
foods.description: Cold Atlantic Walvis Bay waters grow some of the best oysters in the world, exported across Africa and Europe.

foods.name: Boerewors
foods.description: A long thick spiced beef sausage coiled into a spiral, grilled over coals. Best served in a bread roll for a "boerie roll".

foods.name: Vetkoek
foods.description: Pillowy deep-fried dough balls, filled with savoury mince or sweet jam. The southern African road-trip snack.

scavengerItems.label: A 300-metre orange sand dune at Sossusvlei (especially Big Daddy or Dune 45)
Plains zebras grazing on golden grass at Etosha
A giraffe at a waterhole, legs splayed to drink
Desert-adapted elephants in the dry Damaraland riverbeds
Where the Namib desert meets the cold Atlantic at Swakopmund
German colonial architecture (think Black Forest cake shops on the African coast)
The MOST star-filled sky you have ever seen (Namibia has Africa's clearest dark skies)
Black or white rhino in Etosha (Namibia is one of the few places they're increasing)
A quiver tree (a kokerboom), a strange branching plant only growing in southern Namibia
A lappet-faced vulture, the biggest vulture in Africa
A desert-adapted cheetah (Namibia has the world's largest cheetah population)
A Himba village with women in red ochre and traditional dress

geoMatches.description: The capital. Quiet, clean, surrounded by mountains. German colonial buildings sit beside modern African architecture.
The famous orange-dune section of the Namib Desert. Climb Dune 45 at sunrise, walk into Deadvlei (a 900-year-old dead-tree pan), photograph in disbelief.
A massive salt-pan national park where animals come to a handful of waterholes. Elephants, lions, rhinos, giraffes, zebras, all easy to spot.
A small Atlantic coast town that looks like 1900s Bavaria washed up on the desert. Sand-dune adventures, kayaking with seals, fresh seafood.
The wild northern Atlantic coast, named after the shipwrecks and whale bones scattered along it. Desolate, foggy, magnificent.
Red rock country in the north-west, home to desert-adapted elephants, giraffes and lions, and ancient San rock paintings at Twyfelfontein.

senses.smell: Dry desert air, woodsmoke from a braai grill, salt and seaweed on the Atlantic coast, ripe fruit at a Windhoek market, dust after a rare desert rain…
senses.hear: Wind across dunes, "Hallo!" and "Dankie!", a fish eagle calling at Etosha waterholes, the distant rumble of an elephant, the silence of the desert at night…
senses.taste: Smoky game-meat steaks, salty Walvis Bay oysters, sweet jam-filled vetkoek, the surprising richness of springbok pâté, fresh Namibian beef…
senses.feel: Soft warm orange sand, cold Atlantic spray, the chill of a desert dawn followed by the heat of the same day, the rough bark of a quiver tree…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How old is the oldest desert?
stories.body: The Namib Desert has been dry for at least 55 million years (some scientists say up to 80 million), which makes it the OLDEST desert on Earth. The Sahara is "only" about 7 million years old by comparison. The cold Benguela Current flowing up from Antarctica creates a layer of cold dry air over the coast that traps moisture out at sea, so almost no rain ever reaches the inland. Despite that, the Namib has unique life adapted to fog: a beetle (the Namib darkling beetle) that stands on its head to catch fog droplets, plants like the Welwitschia (which can live for 1,500+ years), and even the famous "fairy circles", mysterious round patches of bare ground that scientists still don't fully understand.

stories.question: Why are the dunes orange?
stories.body: The Namib dunes are some of the tallest on Earth (Dune 7 near Walvis Bay is around 380 metres), and they are vivid orange-red. The colour comes from iron in the sand. Over millions of years, the iron particles have oxidised (rusted) in the desert air, coating each grain in red. Younger dunes (near the coast) are more pale yellow, because they're newer. Older dunes (inland) have had longer to rust and are deepest red. Climbing them is exhausting because you slide back half a step for every step up, but the view from the top across the desert at sunrise is something most people never forget.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole landscape is the colour it is because of rusting iron. What other natural colours come from chemistry you didn't expect?

stories.question: Why does so much wildlife gather at Etosha?
stories.body: Etosha is a giant salt pan, about 4,800 square km of mostly dry, blindingly white salt left behind by a vanished lake. It is so dry that most of the year, animals can only drink at a few permanent waterholes around the edge of the pan. In the dry season (May to October), elephants, lions, giraffes, zebras, oryx, springbok and black rhinos all CONCENTRATE at these waterholes. Sit in a hide near a waterhole at dawn and you can see herds in their thousands. Etosha holds one of the last viable wild black rhino populations on Earth.

stories.question: Who are the Himba?
stories.body: The Himba are a semi-nomadic people who live in the dry north-west of Namibia, in Kunene region. They are famous for their distinctive look: women cover their skin and hair in "otjize", a paste of butter fat mixed with red ochre that they make from local rocks. The paste protects them from sun and insects, and gives them their deep red colour. Himba villages are circles of mud-and-dung huts around a central cattle pen with a sacred fire that is never allowed to go out. Visiting a Himba village respectfully (with a local guide who has the family's permission) is one of the most memorable cultural experiences in Namibia.

stories.question: Why do some Namibians speak German?
stories.body: Namibia was a German colony from 1884 to 1915, called "Deutsch-Südwestafrika" (German South West Africa). It was Germany's biggest colony in Africa. After Germany lost World War I, the territory was given to South Africa to administer, and South Africa ran it until Namibian independence in 1990. The German colonial period was very harsh: the 1904-08 wars against the Herero and Nama peoples are now recognised as a genocide. The German legacy you see today is mostly architectural (Swakopmund, parts of Windhoek), and about 1% of Namibians (around 30,000 people) still speak German as a first language. It is a complicated heritage that Namibia is still working through.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Namibia so far?
Dunes, salt pan, coast, mountains, which has felt the most "Namibia"?
Have you slid down a Sossusvlei dune yet?
How big do the night-sky stars actually look here?
Namibia is one of the emptiest countries on Earth. How does that change how the country feels?

wordSearch.hint: Namibian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Hartmann's mountain zebra
animals.description: A desert-adapted zebra with very narrow stripes. Climbs steep dry hills around Damaraland.

animals.name: Giraffe
animals.description: Across Etosha and Damaraland. Sometimes drinking awkwardly with legs splayed.

animals.name: Desert elephant
animals.description: Specially adapted elephants that walk dry riverbeds in Damaraland and Kaokoland. Smaller feet, longer legs than savanna elephants.

animals.name: Lion
animals.description: In Etosha. The desert-adapted lions of the Skeleton Coast region survive on shipwrecked seals and antelope.

animals.name: Black rhino
animals.description: Namibia has the largest free-ranging black rhino population on Earth, in Etosha and protected community conservancies.

animals.name: Lappet-faced vulture
animals.description: Africa's biggest vulture, with a 3 m wingspan. Soaring above Etosha.

animals.name: Cheetah
animals.description: Namibia holds the largest wild cheetah population in the world. Best at Cheetah Conservation Fund near Otjiwarongo.
